Finance can serve that goal only when law and ecology set the boundaries of trade.

Exam-length model

6. Realistic IELTS essay · approximately 300 words

Question: Some people believe governments should create more protected areas, while others argue that conservation should focus on landscapes where people live and work. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
Model answer · 333 words

Governments can conserve biodiversity by expanding protected areas or by restoring ecosystems throughout ordinary landscapes. Both approaches are necessary, but I believe connected restoration beyond reserves is especially important once the most valuable habitats have received strict protection.

Protected areas provide a legal foundation. They can set aside breeding sites, forests and wetlands where destructive activities are restricted. Large reserves support population viability and allow managers to control hunting or invasive species. What makes formal protection essential is that some habitats are too valuable to expose to repeated development pressure. Without strong reserves, restoration elsewhere may simply compensate for continuing loss. However, isolated parks cannot protect species that migrate or move through agricultural and urban land. Roads break up habitat, while pollution and intensive farming cross administrative boundaries. Governments therefore need wildlife corridor, river restoration and nature-friendly management outside reserves. Only when protected areas link up with the wider landscape can populations maintain genetic diversity.

Restoration can also create benefits near where people live. Wetlands reduce flooding, trees cool cities and pollinator habitat supports farming. These outcomes strengthen public confidence because conservation is visible in ordinary communities. Yet restoration must not become an excuse for destroying mature ecosystems and promising replacement later. The best policy follows a hierarchy. Governments should strictly protect irreplaceable sites, improve the management of existing paper parks and restore connectivity across productive landscapes. Many countries have expanded protected-area maps, yet management funding and ecological outcomes have remained weak. Public spending should support rangers, monitoring and community partnerships.

Had earlier transport and housing plans preserved corridors, expensive wildlife crossings might not now be necessary. Planning authorities should therefore map movement routes before approving construction. Developers should fund crossings and long-term monitoring where fragmentation cannot be avoided.

In conclusion, protected areas remain indispensable, but they cannot function as isolated islands. Nature recovery requires strong reserves connected by restoration across farms, rivers, roads and cities. The objective should be a functioning ecological network rather than the largest possible percentage on a map.

Why the exam-length essay is strong

Direct position

The introduction treats protected areas and working landscapes as complementary rather than mutually exclusive.

Causal explanation

The essay connects habitat quality, ecological movement, local livelihoods and enforcement capacity.

Developed contrast

Strict protection for irreplaceable habitats is balanced against coexistence across inhabited landscapes.

Policy mechanism

Corridors, compensation, community governance and credible monitoring turn general support into a workable programme.

Recycled language

Earlier collocations return as part of the reasoning rather than as decoration.

Controlled complexity

Advanced grammar remains clear enough for realistic exam conditions.

7. Advanced grammar transformations

1. If governments protected corridors earlier, fewer populations would now be isolated. (Conditional inversion)

Had governments protected corridors earlier, fewer populations would now be isolated.

2. Developers destroyed the wetland before they assessed its ecological value. (Past-perfect conditional)

Had developers assessed the wetland’s ecological value, they might not have destroyed it.

3. Habitat connectivity matters most for small populations. (Cleft sentence)

What matters most for small populations is habitat connectivity.

4. Wildlife crossings succeed only when they follow real movement routes. (Negative inversion)

Only when wildlife crossings follow real movement routes do they succeed.

5. Protected areas preserve habitat and support ecological processes. (Not only...but also)

Not only do protected areas preserve habitat, but they also support ecological processes.

6. The project was designed for restoration, but it became a marketing exercise. (Participle clause)

Designed for restoration, the project gradually became a marketing exercise.

7. Although rewilding is ambitious, it is not suitable for every landscape. (Fronted concession)

Ambitious though rewilding may be, it is not suitable for every landscape.

8. Governments should protect habitat, finance enforcement and involve communities. (Controlled parallelism)

Governments should protect habitat, finance enforcement and involve communities.

9. Countries have expanded protected areas, but management remains weak. (Present-perfect contrast)

Countries have expanded protected areas, yet management remains weak.

10. The species recovered after the corridor had been restored. (Past perfect)

The species recovered after the corridor had been restored.

11. The baseline lacks accuracy, so the claimed gain is unreliable. (Nominalisation)

The inaccuracy of the baseline makes the claimed gain unreliable.

12. If local people received fair benefits, conservation would gain legitimacy. (Conditional inversion)

Conservation would gain legitimacy were local people to receive fair benefits.

13. Residents opposed the reintroduction because officials ignored conflict risks. (Cleft cause)

It was the failure to address conflict risks that caused opposition to the reintroduction.

14. Policy should protect rare species and maintain common wildlife. (Balanced recommendation)

Policy should both protect rare species and maintain common wildlife.

15. The agency introduced compensation gradually, so communities could test the process. (Participle clause)

Introducing compensation gradually, the agency allowed communities to test the process.

16. Rangers changed patrol routes after new evidence appeared. (Emphatic do)

Rangers did change patrol routes after new evidence appeared.

17. No factor matters more than long-term enforcement. (Negative inversion)

Rarely does any factor matter more than long-term enforcement.

18. The conservation system should be connected, fair and measurable. (Controlled parallelism)

The conservation system should strengthen connectivity, preserve fairness and produce verifiable conservation outcomes.

8. Native Academic Toolbox

1. Upgrade: “Animals cannot move between forests.” using ecological connectivity.

Weak ecological connectivity prevents wildlife populations from moving between forests.

2. Upgrade: “The road divides the habitat.” using habitat fragmentation.

The road causes habitat fragmentation and isolates breeding populations.

3. Upgrade: “The park exists only on a map.” using paper parks.

Paper parks provide formal designation without effective management or enforcement.

4. Upgrade: “The project wants nature to recover naturally.” using natural regeneration.

Natural regeneration allows ecosystems to recover with limited direct intervention.

5. Upgrade: “A species is returned to an old habitat.” using species reintroduction.

Species reintroduction returns a locally extinct population to suitable habitat.

6. Upgrade: “The developer creates habitat somewhere else.” using biodiversity offsetting.

Biodiversity offsetting compensates for residual ecological damage at another site.

7. Upgrade: “The starting condition was measured badly.” using ecological baseline.

An inaccurate ecological baseline can exaggerate the scale of reported improvement.

8. Upgrade: “Local people manage the protected area.” using community conservation.

Community conservation gives residents a direct role in protection and benefit-sharing.

9. Upgrade: “The project needs money for many years.” using conservation finance.

Stable conservation finance is essential for long-term monitoring and enforcement.

10. Upgrade: “Road crossings help animals move safely.” using wildlife crossings.

Wildlife crossings reconnect habitat and reduce vehicle-related mortality.

11. Upgrade: “The species is important to the whole ecosystem.” using keystone species.

A keystone species has an ecological influence disproportionate to its abundance.

12. Upgrade: “The law must be enforced in the field.” using conservation enforcement.

Conservation enforcement turns formal protection into practical limits on harmful activity.

13. Upgrade: “The ecosystem gives people useful benefits.” using ecosystem services.

Ecosystem services include pollination, flood regulation and water purification.

14. Upgrade: “The project helps nature overall.” using nature-positive development.

Nature-positive development should create verified ecological recovery rather than vague improvement claims.

15. Upgrade: “The protected sites need to connect with each other.” using wildlife corridor.

A wildlife corridor links protected sites and supports movement and genetic exchange.

9. IELTS Speaking

Part 1 · 15 questions

PART 1 · 1

Do you enjoy visiting natural areas?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move throughbuild up
Model answer · 83 words

I enjoy visiting forests, wetlands and coastal areas because the experience is quieter and less controlled than an ordinary park. Walking slowly helps me move through the landscape without treating it as a background for photographs. Over time, these visits build up my awareness of seasonal change and species abundance. I also notice how paths, roads and litter affect wildlife habitat, which makes conservation feel practical rather than abstract. Local guides can also explain ecological details that a casual visitor would otherwise miss.

PART 1 · 2

Have you ever seen a wild animal near your home?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move throughcut down on
Model answer · 81 words

I occasionally see foxes, birds and hedgehogs near residential streets. They often move through gardens because continuous natural habitat is limited. Better planting, smaller fence gaps and slower traffic could cut down on road mortality. Encounters are enjoyable, but people should avoid feeding wildlife heavily because dependence and conflict can follow. Urban nature works best when animals can use connected spaces without becoming permanent attractions around one house. Small design choices across many properties can create a surprisingly useful urban corridor.

PART 1 · 3

Would you volunteer for a conservation project?

Suggested phrasal verbs
carry outbuild up
Model answer · 85 words

I would volunteer for a well-organised project involving surveys, litter removal or habitat management. Volunteers can carry out useful tasks, but they need training so that enthusiasm does not damage sensitive sites. Repeated participation could build up practical knowledge and support species monitoring. I would prefer a project that shares its findings with volunteers, because people remain engaged when they understand how one morning’s work contributes to a wider restoration plan. Clear safety rules also protect volunteers and the species they are trying to help.

PART 1 · 4

Do you like wildlife documentaries?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring backdie out
Model answer · 80 words

I enjoy wildlife documentaries when they explain ecological relationships rather than showing only dramatic predators. The strongest programmes demonstrate how conservation can bring back a missing species or why a population may die out after habitat becomes fragmented. Beautiful images are valuable, but they can create the impression that nature remains abundant everywhere. Good documentaries connect animal behaviour with habitat loss, climate pressure and decisions made by governments and consumers. This makes the programme informative rather than merely emotionally impressive.

PART 1 · 5

Are there protected areas near where you live?

Suggested phrasal verbs
set asidelink up
Model answer · 82 words

There are several small protected areas within travelling distance, although they are separated by roads and development. Governments can set aside land, but isolated sites are less useful if animals cannot move between them. Hedgerows, riverbanks and wildlife crossings could link up these places. Management quality also matters because a label on a map does not guarantee funding, rangers or healthy habitat. Some apparently protected landscapes still require substantial restoration. Public access should be managed so recreation does not damage breeding areas.

PART 1 · 6

Would you support a wildlife bridge over a road?

Suggested phrasal verbs
link upcut down on
Model answer · 82 words

I would support a green bridge where a major road divides important habitat. The structure can link up animal populations and cut down on collisions with vehicles. It should use local vegetation and fencing that guides wildlife towards the crossing. Monitoring is essential because an attractive bridge is not automatically effective. If cameras and surveys show that several species use it, the project could become a model for future transport schemes. The road project should fund long-term maintenance rather than only construction.

PART 1 · 7

Do you plant flowers for insects?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring backbuild up
Model answer · 82 words

I try to grow a few native flowers that provide pollen and shelter. One garden cannot reverse pollinator decline, but many connected gardens can bring back food sources across a neighbourhood. Diverse planting also build up soil biodiversity and supports birds. I avoid pesticides where possible and leave some stems during winter. The result is less tidy than a decorative lawn, but a perfectly controlled garden often offers very little ecological value. Neighbouring balconies and public verges can extend the same network.

PART 1 · 8

Would you visit a rewilding project?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring backwind down
Model answer · 81 words

I would visit a rewilding project because it would be interesting to see land managed through natural processes rather than constant landscaping. Reintroducing grazers or beavers may bring back structural diversity and improve water systems. The investment can bear fruit through nature tourism, flood reduction and healthier ecosystems. However, I would want the project to involve nearby farmers and residents instead of presenting local concerns as obstacles to an idealised wilderness. Reliable evidence matters because rewilding should be evaluated, not worshipped.

PART 1 · 9

Do you think zoos help conservation?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring backcarry out
Model answer · 83 words

Zoos can help when they carry out credible breeding, research and public education. Captive programmes may support efforts to bring back species whose wild populations are extremely small. However, displaying common animals in poor conditions is not conservation. Institutions should publish welfare standards, financial contributions and release outcomes. Habitat protection usually matters more than breeding animals that have nowhere safe to return, so zoos should not become an excuse to neglect ecosystems. Conservation claims should be independently checked rather than treated as marketing.

PART 1 · 10

Have you heard of invasive species?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring instep in
Model answer · 86 words

I have heard of plants, insects and animals that were introduced outside their natural range and then spread rapidly. Governments may need to bring in controls before an invasive species becomes established. Delayed action can get in the way of ecological restoration because native species continue to face competition or disease. The issue requires care, since not every non-native organism is harmful. Policy should focus on measurable ecological damage rather than emotional language about foreign species. Prevention is usually cheaper than attempting eradication after widespread establishment.

PART 1 · 11

Would you pay more for nature-friendly products?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bear fruitcarry out
Model answer · 84 words

I would pay moderately more when a certification system is credible and transparent. Producers need incentives to wind down practices that destroy habitat and ramp up methods that protect water, soil and wildlife. The price difference must remain affordable, because responsibility cannot rest only on wealthy consumers. Government procurement and regulation can shift entire markets more effectively than asking individuals to investigate every supply chain while standing in a supermarket aisle. Clear labelling could help consumers compare real outcomes instead of vague green claims.

PART 1 · 12

Do local people benefit from national parks?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build upcarry out
Model answer · 79 words

They can benefit through conservation jobs, tourism and improved ecosystem services. Protection may bear fruit locally when residents receive training, revenue and decision-making power. It can also build up resentment if land-use restrictions are imposed without compensation. local communities should therefore help manage parks and share financial benefits. A successful reserve protects wildlife while recognising that neighbouring households need livelihoods, infrastructure and a meaningful voice. Conservation works best when residents become partners rather than displaced spectators. Local knowledge matters.

PART 1 · 13

Would you report illegal wildlife trade?

Suggested phrasal verbs
carry outstep in
Model answer · 82 words

I would report suspicious wildlife products or online sales to the relevant authority rather than confronting anyone directly. Trained officers can carry out an investigation and step in when evidence is sufficient. wildlife trafficking is often connected to organised crime and weak enforcement, so individual action needs institutional support. Public information should explain which products are illegal, because consumers may not realise that souvenirs, pets or medicines involve threatened species. Platforms should also remove illegal listings quickly and preserve evidence for investigators.

PART 1 · 14

Do you prefer tidy parks or wilder parks?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build upmove through
Model answer · 88 words

I prefer a balance. Paths and seating make a park accessible, while wilder areas build up habitat complexity. Insects, birds and small mammals need dead wood, long grass and dense vegetation. People can still move through the park safely if routes are clearly maintained. A completely manicured landscape may look orderly but support few species. Good design explains why some areas are left untidy, which can prevent residents from assuming that managers simply stopped working. Seasonal mowing can keep sightlines open while allowing flowers and insects to recover.

PART 1 · 15

Should children learn about biodiversity?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring backcarry out
Model answer · 85 words

Children should learn how food webs, pollination and habitat connections work. Schools can carry out simple surveys and compare species across seasons. Restoration stories show that communities can bring back wildlife rather than only documenting decline. Lessons should also discuss difficult choices involving farming, housing and predators. Biodiversity education becomes meaningful when students investigate local places and see that nature is a functioning system, not a list of exotic animals from distant documentaries. Field visits could connect classroom knowledge with visible changes in local habitats.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

Why is biodiversity important to society?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build upbring back
Model answer · 126 words

Biodiversity supports food production, water regulation, soil formation and disease control. Healthy ecosystems build up resilience because several species can perform overlapping functions when conditions change. Conservation can also bear fruit economically through fisheries, tourism and reduced flood damage. However, nature should not be valued only when one service has an immediate market price. ecosystem services explain part of the relationship, while cultural meaning and the continued existence of species matter independently. cost-benefit analysis should therefore inform policy without pretending every ecological loss can be converted into a precise financial figure. These benefits also reduce public-health and infrastructure costs that rarely appear in conservation budgets. Biodiversity also provides option value because future medicines, crops and technologies may depend on species whose importance is not yet understood.

PART 3 · 2

Is protecting 30 percent of land and sea enough?

Suggested phrasal verbs
set asidelink up
Model answer · 124 words

The thirty-by-thirty target is useful because it creates a measurable global ambition, but percentage alone is insufficient. Governments can set aside remote or low-conflict areas while permitting destruction in places with greater ecological value. Protected sites must also link up through corridors, rivers and coastal networks. Management quality, representation and community rights matter as much as area. A country could reach thirty percent on paper while maintaining several paper parks. The target should therefore be treated as a floor combined with ecological connectivity, effective enforcement and restoration outside formal reserves. The target must protect ecologically representative areas, not merely the cheapest land or sea to designate. Governments should publish management effectiveness, funding and connectivity data so area targets cannot be met through statistical theatre.

PART 3 · 3

Should endangered species receive priority over common species?

Suggested phrasal verbs
die outbring back
Model answer · 126 words

Endangered species deserve urgent attention because delay may allow them to die out, and extinction is irreversible. Targeted breeding or habitat protection can sometimes bring back small populations. However, focusing only on rare species can ignore declining common wildlife and the ecosystems that support them. Conservation should protect processes, habitats and species abundance as well as charismatic animals. A common pollinator or soil organism may provide greater ecological function than a famous predator. Priority setting should combine extinction risk, ecological role, feasibility and wider habitat benefit rather than allowing public popularity to determine every funding decision. Preventing broad declines is usually cheaper than emergency action after a species becomes critically rare. Conservation policy should therefore intervene before warning signs become dramatic enough to attract emergency funding.

PART 3 · 4

How can roads be made safer for wildlife?

Suggested phrasal verbs
break upcut down on
Model answer · 119 words

Roads break up habitat, restrict migration and expose animals to vehicles. Planners can cut down on these impacts through tunnels, fencing, reduced speed and carefully located wildlife crossings. A green bridge should recreate local vegetation and connect genuine movement routes rather than being added where construction is easiest. Monitoring through cameras and genetic surveys can test whether populations reconnect. Transport budgets should include ecological mitigation from the beginning. Retrofitting is possible, but it is usually more expensive and less effective than avoiding fragmentation during route selection. Green infrastructure should be treated as part of transport safety, not as optional decoration. Maintenance funding is essential because fencing, vegetation and monitoring determine whether crossings remain functional decades after construction. Placement matters.

PART 3 · 5

Is rewilding better than traditional conservation?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring backbuild up
Model answer · 124 words

Rewilding can bring back natural processes and build up complex habitats with less continuous management. Large grazers, predators or beavers may create diversity that machinery cannot easily reproduce. Traditional conservation remains necessary where rare species depend on specific conditions or landscapes have been altered beyond spontaneous recovery. The two approaches are therefore complementary. Rewilding is strongest at landscape scale, while targeted management can protect vulnerable remnants. Projects also need clear social safeguards, because reducing intervention for nature may still require active decisions about livestock, access and neighbouring farms. Monitoring should compare rewilding with other management approaches rather than assuming one philosophy fits every landscape. Farmers and residents should help define acceptable outcomes, since landscape recovery is also a long-term change in local land use.

PART 3 · 6

Should developers be allowed to offset habitat destruction?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring inbuild up
Model answer · 128 words

Offsetting may help finance restoration when some damage is unavoidable. Governments can bring in net biodiversity gain rules that require developers to compensate beyond the original loss. Yet weak baselines and distant habitat banks can get in the way of real protection by making destruction appear reversible. Ancient woodland, mature wetlands and culturally important places cannot be recreated quickly elsewhere. The mitigation hierarchy should prioritise avoidance, then reduction, and use biodiversity offsetting only as a last resort. Independent monitoring and long legal commitments are necessary because ecological promises often extend beyond a developer’s involvement. Developers should finance enforcement so local authorities are not left supervising decades of promises without staff. Where restoration fails, developers should remain financially liable rather than transferring the risk to taxpayers or future councils.

PART 3 · 7

How should conservation finance be increased?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring inwind down
Model answer · 127 words

Countries need to ramp up conservation finance from public budgets, development banks, philanthropy and carefully regulated markets. They should wind down subsidies that reward deforestation, overfishing or destructive agriculture. New instruments such as biodiversity credits may attract private capital, but ecological claims require strong verification. Finance should reach rangers, Indigenous organisations and local conservation groups rather than disappearing into complex international administration. Stable multi-year funding is especially important because habitats and institutions cannot recover through a series of short pilots. Public money should also support monitoring, enforcement and enforcement capability, not merely visible restoration ceremonies. Debt and fiscal constraints must also be considered because biodiversity-rich countries may have the least budgetary space. International funds should simplify applications and provide direct access for organisations with demonstrated local capacity.

PART 3 · 8

Can conservation succeed without local communities?

Suggested phrasal verbs
step incarry out
Model answer · 119 words

Conservation rarely succeeds when local people are excluded. Governments may step in to restrict harmful activity, but residents often carry out daily stewardship, monitoring and conflict prevention. If protected areas remove access to land without alternatives, they can create resentment and illegal use. community conservation works best when rights, revenue and responsibilities are clear. Communities should not be romanticised as automatically united or environmentally perfect, yet their knowledge and long-term presence are indispensable. Effective policy combines local authority with scientific support, legal safeguards and mechanisms for resolving internal inequality. Benefit-sharing should include women, young people and groups that may be excluded from formal village leadership. Independent grievance procedures are needed when conservation decisions restrict access or distribute benefits unfairly.

PART 3 · 9

How should human-wildlife conflict be managed?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move throughstep in
Model answer · 118 words

Conflict increases when animals move through farms or settlements after habitat has been fragmented. Authorities should step in with early warnings, fencing, compensation and rapid response teams. Prevention is more effective than punishing communities after crops, livestock or lives have been lost. Corridors can direct movement, while land-use planning reduces risky development. Compensation must be timely and credible, otherwise residents bear the cost of a public conservation goal. Long-term solutions should support livelihoods and local decision-making, because tolerance cannot be demanded indefinitely from people who receive danger but few benefits. Insurance and community-based response teams can complement compensation where losses are frequent. Education and transparent incident data can also reduce exaggerated fear while preserving honest discussion of danger.

PART 3 · 10

Are protected areas compatible with economic development?

Suggested phrasal verbs
set asidestep in
Model answer · 128 words

Protected areas can support development when they safeguard water, tourism, fisheries and climate resilience. Land set aside for nature may bear fruit through benefits that conventional accounts overlook. However, restrictions can create real foregone land-use value for farmers, fishers or communities. Development and conservation are compatible only when these costs are recognised and benefits are shared. Infrastructure should avoid high-value habitat, while sustainable enterprises may operate in buffer zones. The objective is not to freeze every landscape, but to maintain ecological function and prevent short-term extraction from destroying the foundations of future economic activity. Nature tourism can help, but dependency on visitors leaves conservation vulnerable to economic shocks. Protected areas should be integrated with regional planning so economic activity does not isolate them behind expanding roads and settlements.

PART 3 · 11

Should conservation focus on ecosystems or individual species?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring backlink up
Model answer · 124 words

Ecosystem-focused policy usually delivers broader protection because species depend on food webs, hydrology and connected habitat. Restoring wetlands or forests may bring back many organisms simultaneously. Corridors can link up populations across large landscapes. Nevertheless, some species require targeted action, especially when numbers are extremely low or threats are specific. A combined strategy is best: protect ecological processes while using recovery plans for the most vulnerable species. This also reduces the risk that conservation becomes a competition among attractive animals while less visible plants, fungi and freshwater species continue to decline. Recovery plans should include genetic management when isolated populations remain too small for long-term viability. Targeted intervention may be temporary, but it must continue until the wider ecosystem can support the recovering species.

PART 3 · 12

What are the risks of reintroducing predators?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring backstep in
Model answer · 121 words

Predator reintroduction may bring back missing ecological relationships, but it can also affect livestock, hunting and public safety perceptions. Authorities must step in with careful site selection, compensation and long-term monitoring. A scientific ecological risk appraisal should examine prey availability, connectivity and likely conflict. structured community deliberation needs to occur before release rather than after opposition becomes organised. Predators should not be introduced simply because the project creates international attention. Success depends on ecological suitability, locally grounded legitimacy and the capacity to manage unexpected movement beyond the original area. Independent review is especially important where political prestige may pressure agencies to declare success prematurely. Release plans should include clear thresholds for pausing or reversing the programme when evidence changes. Context matters.

PART 3 · 13

Can biodiversity markets be trusted?

Suggested phrasal verbs
bring incut down on
Model answer · 118 words

Markets can ramp up finance, but they cannot be trusted without strict rules. Governments need to bring in a conservation-governance framework covering baselines, additionality, permanence and double counting. Biodiversity is more difficult to standardise than carbon because habitats and species are location-specific. A restored grassland cannot automatically compensate for a destroyed wetland. Credits may support useful projects, yet they should supplement public funding and strong law. Independent audits, public registries and long monitoring periods are necessary to prevent nature-positive development from becoming a polished label for ordinary damage. Public buyers and financial institutions should disclose whether credits replace stronger avoidance measures. Markets should never determine whether irreplaceable habitat may be destroyed; some losses require an absolute legal limit.

PART 3 · 14

Why do conservation projects sometimes fail?

Suggested phrasal verbs
carry outmove through
Model answer · 128 words

Projects fail when goals are vague, funding ends early or institutions lack capacity. Weak community engagement can get in the way of implementation, while teams may carry out planting without addressing the original cause of degradation. Invasive species, poor maintenance and unrealistic baselines also undermine outcomes. Conservation works over long timescales, but political programmes often demand rapid visible results. Failure should not be hidden; transparent monitoring can distinguish a useful experiment from repeated negligence. Strong projects define ecological outcomes, secure long-term finance and adapt methods when evidence shows that the original plan is not working. Lessons should be published so other projects do not repeat the same errors under a new brand. Long-term institutional memory matters because staff turnover can erase practical knowledge even when formal reports remain.

PART 3 · 15

What would successful nature recovery look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build upmove through
Model answer · 127 words

Successful recovery would involve rising species abundance, stronger ecological processes and landscapes through which wildlife can move through safely. Protected areas would build up healthy populations while farms, towns and infrastructure reduced pressure beyond their boundaries. Local communities would receive jobs, revenue and decision-making authority. Monitoring would show not merely more planted trees but improved survival, genetic diversity and functioning food webs. Recovery also requires social durability: laws, finance and public support must survive political change. Nature would not return to an imagined untouched past, but ecosystems would become more diverse, connected and capable of adapting. Success would also mean fewer emergency listings because common species remain common. Nature recovery should become ordinary land-use policy rather than a temporary project dependent on one enthusiastic minister or donor.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Developers should be required to produce a measurable net gain in biodiversity whenever new construction damages nature. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
net biodiversity gainhabitat restorationecological baselinebiodiversity offsettinglink upbiodiversity lossecosystem collapsehabitat fragmentationecological connectivity
Model essay · 333 words

Construction frequently destroys habitat, so requiring a measurable net biodiversity gain appears fair. I agree with this policy in principle, provided that it follows strict rules and does not turn compensation into automatic permission for destruction.

The main advantage is that developers must recognise ecological cost. They may redesign projects to avoid sensitive areas or finance habitat restoration. A clear requirement can also create stable demand for ecologists and long-term land management. What the policy changes is the assumption that habitat can be removed without appearing in the project’s financial calculation. However, measurement is difficult. An ecological baseline may be understated, and newly created habitat may not replace a mature ecosystem. biodiversity offsetting is especially weak when compensation occurs far from the affected community. Only when avoidance has been exhausted should off-site compensation be accepted.

Governments should establish no-go habitats, independent surveys and public registries. Developers must remain liable for outcomes over several decades. If promised habitat fails, additional restoration or financial penalties should follow. Local authorities also need funding to inspect sites rather than receiving legal duties without staff. The policy should consider connectivity and quality, not only numerical units. A small project that link up two reserves may be more valuable than a larger isolated habitat bank. Community access and flood protection should also form part of evaluation.

A further safeguard is transparency about unavoidable loss. Planning documents should show which habitats were avoided, reduced or compensated. This allows citizens and researchers to determine whether developers selected the least damaging option. Credits should never replace criminal or civil liability for illegal destruction. The policy can also improve cross-policy alignment if transport, housing and conservation authorities use the same ecological maps. Otherwise, one agency may finance a corridor while another approves development that blocks it.

In conclusion: net-gain requirements can improve development and generate conservation finance. Their value depends on strong avoidance rules, conservative metrics and long-term enforcement. Without these safeguards, a technical calculation may simply make ecological destruction easier to approve.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe rewilding is the best way to restore ecosystems, while others prefer active management for particular species. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
bring backtrophic recoverydie outhuman-wildlife conflictspecies abundancebiodiversity lossecosystem collapsehabitat fragmentationecological connectivity
Model essay · 306 words

Rewilding aims to restore natural processes, whereas traditional conservation often manages habitat for selected species. Both approaches have strengths, and I believe they should be combined according to ecological context.

Rewilding can bring back missing grazers, predators or wetland engineers. These species may create varied habitat and trophic recovery more effectively than repeated mechanical intervention. Once processes are restored, ecosystems can become more self-sustaining. What makes rewilding attractive is its ambition to recover ecological relationships rather than preserve a static landscape. However, rare species may depend on conditions that no longer arise naturally. Small grasslands may require mowing, while nesting birds may need predator control. Waiting for spontaneous recovery could allow a vulnerable population to die out. Reintroduced animals can also create human-wildlife conflict if surrounding communities are ignored.

The appropriate strategy is landscape zoning. Large connected areas may support rewilding, while high-value remnants receive targeted management. Scientists should establish baselines and monitor species abundance, habitat structure and social effects. Not only should projects restore ecological function, but they should also maintain local legitimacy. Farmers and residents need compensation, consultation and opportunities through tourism or conservation employment. Rewilding should not be imposed as though people living in the landscape are an unfortunate technical complication.

The distinction also depends on scale. A small urban reserve cannot become a self-regulating wilderness, while a large landscape may be damaged by constant attempts to preserve one historical snapshot. Management should respond to changing climate conditions and allow learning rather than treating one philosophy as permanent doctrine. Independent review is useful because organisations may become emotionally or financially committed to declaring their preferred method successful.

In conclusion: rewilding is a powerful method but not a universal replacement for active conservation. The best approach restores natural processes where possible and uses targeted management where specific species or habitats still depend on it.

TASK 2 · 3

Governments are building wildlife bridges and tunnels across major roads. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
break upgenetic diversitygreen bridgelink upcut down onroad mortalitywildlife corridorbiodiversity lossecosystem collapse
Model essay · 302 words

Roads connect communities and economies, but they also divide habitats and kill wildlife. Green bridges and tunnels are increasingly used to reduce these impacts. I believe their advantages generally outweigh their costs when they are located and monitored scientifically.

The ecological benefit is connectivity. Roads break up populations, reduce genetic diversity and prevent seasonal movement. A green bridge or tunnel can link up habitat and cut down on road mortality. These structures may support mammals, reptiles, insects and plants rather than one flagship animal. They can also improve human safety by reducing collisions with large wildlife. What appears to be conservation infrastructure can therefore function as road-safety infrastructure as well. Building a crossing during road construction may represent only a small share of the total budget.

The disadvantages are cost and uncertainty. A badly located crossing may receive little use, while fencing and vegetation require maintenance. Governments may also build one photogenic bridge while approving wider habitat destruction. Were long-term monitoring omitted, officials could claim success without evidence. Planning should identify movement routes before construction. Camera traps, track surveys and genetic monitoring can evaluate use. Road agencies should fund maintenance for the full life of the infrastructure and integrate crossings into wider wildlife corridor.

Crossings should be accompanied by avoidance. A new bridge cannot justify routing a motorway through irreplaceable habitat when a less damaging route exists. Environmental assessment should compare the entire project, not merely celebrate the mitigation feature. The structures may also help wildlife respond to climate change by enabling movement towards more suitable habitat. This long-term function can be more important than the immediate reduction in vehicle deaths.

In conclusion: wildlife crossings provide ecological and safety benefits that usually justify their expense. Their success depends on strategic location, supporting fencing and commitment to long-term monitoring rather than symbolic construction.

TASK 2 · 4

Human-wildlife conflict is increasing in many rural areas. What problems does this cause, and what solutions should governments introduce?

Optional collocation bank
break upmove throughstep inbiodiversity lossecosystem collapsehabitat fragmentationecological connectivitywildlife corridorgreen bridge
Model essay · 322 words

As farms and settlements expand into wildlife habitat, encounters between people and animals are becoming more frequent. Conflict can damage livelihoods, threaten safety and weaken support for conservation.

Farmers may lose crops or livestock, while large animals can injure people. Residents who receive no compensation may kill wildlife or oppose nearby protected areas. The problem is intensified when roads and development break up migration routes, forcing animals to move through settlements. What conservation policy often underestimates is the daily cost borne by people living beside wildlife. Governments should invest in prevention. Corridors, fencing, livestock enclosures and early-warning systems can reduce dangerous encounters. Rapid-response teams should step in before one incident escalates. Land-use planning must also avoid construction on established movement routes.

Compensation is essential but must be prompt and simple. Delayed payments destroy institutional trust. Insurance or community funds may work where losses are frequent. Not only should households receive payment, but they should also share income from tourism and conservation employment. Education can reduce exaggerated fear, although it cannot replace material protection. Local communities should help design rules and monitor animal movement. Where risk remains unacceptable, authorities may need relocation, population management or changes to crop choice.

Reliable data can improve prevention. GPS collars, community reports and seasonal maps may show where animals approach farms. However, sensitive location data requires careful data governance so it is not misused by poachers. Policies should also address the causes of habitat pressure. If commercial agriculture or poorly planned roads continue narrowing corridors, compensation treats symptoms while conflict expands. Governments should publish conflict data and compensation outcomes, because repeated incidents may reveal where corridor restoration or land-use change is more effective than paying for losses indefinitely. This also improves public accountability and allows local communities to compare promised support with actual delivery.

In conclusion: human-wildlife conflict threatens both livelihoods and conservation legitimacy. Prevention, credible compensation and community participation can reduce harm while preserving ecological connectivity.

TASK 2 · 5

Why is international conservation finance insufficient? How can more funding reach local organisations and communities?

Optional collocation bank
conservation financeanti-poaching patrolsconservation jobsbiodiversity lossecosystem collapsehabitat fragmentationecological connectivitywildlife corridorgreen bridge
Model essay · 308 words

International conservation commitments are ambitious, yet many local organisations face unstable and inadequate funding. This gap results from weak political incentives, complex finance systems and the unequal distribution of institutional power.

Biodiversity protection often produces global benefits but local costs. Wealthy countries may support international targets without providing the promised conservation finance. Donors also prefer short projects with visible results, whereas ranger salaries, enforcement and monitoring require long commitments. What funding systems reward is often administrative capacity rather than ecological need. Small organisations struggle with complex applications, audits and English-language reporting. Money may pass through several international institutions before reaching the field. Sudden cuts then stop anti-poaching patrols and eliminate conservation jobs, even where projects had strong local support.

Developed countries should ramp up grant-based finance and meet agreed international commitments. Funds need direct-access routes for organisations with proven local capacity. Multi-year grants should cover salaries, equipment and institutional development, not only activities attractive to donors. Transparency is also necessary. Public databases should show how much money reaches local implementation. Had earlier programmes invested more consistently in local institutions, abrupt donor withdrawal might have caused less damage.

Communities should control part of the budget and participate in monitoring. However, safeguards are needed to ensure women, younger residents and marginalised households share benefits. New sources can complement government aid. Debt arrangements, philanthropy and regulated biodiversity markets may support conservation, but they should not substitute for public responsibility. Private funding often favours attractive landscapes and species, leaving less visible ecosystems underfunded. Local groups also need flexibility. Emergencies such as fire, conflict or wildlife disease cannot wait for approval through a distant annual work plan.

In conclusion: conservation finance is insufficient because global benefits are politically distant and access systems favour large organisations. More predictable commitments, simpler applications and direct local funding would make conservation both more effective and more legitimate.
